summary: Joel Spolsky announces stackoverflow.com, a free programming Q&A site he's building with Jeff Atwood. He observes that programmers have largely stopped reading books and instead rely on Google, only to find answers locked behind paywalls on sites that offer mediocre, unvetted responses. Stack Overflow will be completely free to use, with no cloaking or pay gates, built on plain HTML. Unusually for Joel, he's announcing the project before it ships because it's a community effort that needs programmer input from the start. Jeff Atwood will serve as full-time CEO while Joel remains at Fog Creek in a consulting role.
key_points: The market for programming books has collapsed relative to the number of working programmers, replaced by trial-and-error and Google searches
Existing programming Q&A sites hide answers behind paywalls and use manipulative tactics like cloaking and rot13 text, despite offering non-expert answers
Stack Overflow will be completely free with no pay gates, built on plain HTML, with optional registration for karma and flair
Joel breaks his usual rule of keeping quiet until shipping because this is a community project that needs public input during design and development
The project is a joint venture between Fog Creek and Jeff Atwood, with Atwood as full-time CEO
money_quotes: Programmers seem to have stopped reading books. The market for books on programming topics is miniscule compared to the number of working programmers.
Instead, they happily program away, using trial-and-error. When they can't figure something out, they type a question into Google.

And sometimes, the first result looks like it's going to have the answer to their exact question, and they are excited, until they click on the link, and discover that it's a pay site, and the answer is cloaked or hidden or behind a pay-wall.
It's hard to imagine that with the cooperative spirit of the internet this is the best thing we programmers have come up with.
Free to ask questions, free to answer questions, free to read, free to index, built with plain old HTML, no fake rot13 text on the home page, no scammy google-cloaking tactics, no salespeople.
You can register if you want to collect karma and win valuable flair that will appear next to your name, but otherwise, it's just free.
When I'm building a new product, my policy has always been to keep quiet about it until I have something to ship. But this isn't really a product.
key_insight: The existing ecosystem of paywalled, low-quality programming Q&A sites represents a market failure that a free, community-driven alternative can solve by aligning incentives with the cooperative spirit of the internet.
